content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA 
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 
1 1102 Q Street • Suite 3000 • Sacramento, CA 95811 
(•11<>) ;t.!:.?·!i<,611 • Fax (916) :l:.!:.!-088(1 
Mark J. Hattam 
General Counsel 
San Diego County Water Authority 
4677 Overland Avenue 
San Diego, CA 92123-1233 
Re: 
Your Request for Advice 
Our File No. A-17-174 
Dear Mr. Hattam: 
October 23 2017 
This letter responds to your request for advice on behalf of San Diego County Water 
Authority ("SDCW A") Director Kathleen Coates Hedberg regarding the conflict of interest 
provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act") 1 and Government Code Section 1090. Please 
note that we do not advise on any other area of law, including Public Contract Code or common law 
conflicts of interest. We are also not a finder of fact when rendering advice (/11 re Oglesby ( 1975) 1 
FPPC Ops. 71), and any advice we provide assumes your facts are complete and accurate. 
In regard to our advice on Section I 090, we are required to forward your request and all 
pertinent facts relating to the request to the Attorney General's Office and the San Diego County 
District Attorney's Office, which we have done. (Section 1097.l(c)(3).) We did not receive a 
written response from either entity. (Section 1097.l(c)(4).) We are also required to advise you that, 
for purposes of Section 1090, the following advice "is not admissible in a criminal proceeding 
against any individual other than the requestor." (See Section 1097.l(c)(S).) 
QUESTION 
Under Section 1090 and the Act, may Director Coates Hedberg participate in and vote on 
any upcoming SDCW A contracts with San Diego Gas and Electric ("SDG&E"), given that her 
spouse is employed by SDG&E, and owns Sempra Energy ("Sempra" is the parent of SDG&E) 
stock in a 401(k) plan? 
CONCLUSION 
No. Under Section 1090, Director Coates Hedberg has a financial interest in the contract. 
However, because it is a "remote interest," SDCW A may enter into such contracts. Director Coates 
Hedberg may not make or participate in any decisions involving the contracts. Additionally, 
1 The Political Refonn Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory 
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices 
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All 
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated. 


File No. A-17-174 
Page No. 2 
because the remedy in this situation under the Act would be the same, we do not analyze the 
conflicts of interest under the Act. 
FACTS 
Director Coates Hedberg is an elected representative of the Helix Water District ("Helix"), 
and was appointed to the SDCWA Board as Helix's representative in December 2016. SDCWA is a 
public agency formed pursuant to the County Water Authority Act. 
SDG&E, a public utility that distributes gas and electricity to retail customers in San Diego 
County, is wholly owned by Sempra, a Fortune 500 energy based holding company. Sempra owns 
and operates electric and gas utilities in the U.S. and South America, including SDG&E in 
California. It is a publicly traded company registered with the Securities and Exchange Commission 
and registered under the New York Stock Exchange. SDG&E is the main provider of gas and 
electric services in the San Diego region. SDCW A does business with SDG&E on various energy 
and conservation matters by entering into contracts with SDG&E for power purchase agreements, 
conservation and rebate agreements, grants of easements, and other matters related to energy and 
water. You anticipate there will be a number of future contracts between SDCW A and SDG&E. 
Since SDG&E is the major supplier of gas and electricity in this region, the contracts are generally 
not bid, but negotiated between the parties. 
Director Coates Hedberg's spouse has been an employee ofSDG&E since 1999. He holds 
the position of Computer Engineer with SDG&E. He receives a salary from SDG&E and 
participates in a matching 401(k) defined benefit plan where stock shares and dividends to him are 
contributed into his 40l(k) Plan. This contribution of stock and dividends is valued at over $2,000. 
These Sempra stock shares are less than three percent of the corporation's shares. The salary of 
Director Coates Hedberg's spouse, even without any consideration of dividends, is more than 5% of 
their combined income. Director Coates Hedberg is a SDWCA public official with a community 
property interest in her spouse's income, stock shares, and dividends from SDG&E. 
You note that SDG&E has over 4,000 employees, and that Director Coates Hedberg's 
spouse has been employed by SDG&E for at least 16 years prior to her joining the SDCW A Board. 
In a telephone conversation on October l 0, 2017, you stated that the spouse has no role in 
formulating contracts on behalf of SDG&E, as he is not an officer or director of SDG&E. 
ANALYSIS 
Government Code Section 1090: 
Under Section l 090, "the prohibited act is the making of a contract in which the official has 
a financial interest." (People v. Honig (1996) 48 Cal.App.4th 289, 333.) A contract that violates 
Section 1090 is void. (Thomson v. Call (1985) 38 Cal.3d 633, 646.) Section 1090 is concerned with 
financial interests, other than remote or noninterests, that prevent public officials from exercising 
absolute loyalty and undivided allegiance in furthering the best interests of their agencies. (Stigall v. 
Taft ( 1962) 58 Cal.2d 565, 569.) The analysis of a financial interest under Section 1090 is separate 
and distinct from that applied under the Act. 


File No. A-17-174 
Page No. 3 
We employ a six-step analysis to determine whether an official has a disqualifying conflict 
of interest under Section 1090. These steps are: ( 1) does the matter involve a public officer or 
employee; (2) does the matter involve a contract; (3) is the official making or participating in 
making a contract; (4) does the official have a financial interest in the contract; (5) does a remote or 
noninterest exception apply; and, under appropriate facts, (6) does the rule of necessity apply? 
Steps one through four are not at issue here. This matter involves the Director in her role as 
a public officer, and various contractual agreements with SDG&E concerning energy and water. 
The Director would be making or participating in making of the agreements, because an officer is 
conclusively presumed to be involved in the making of his or her agency's contracts when the 
officer is a member of a board or commission that has the power to execute the contract at issue. 
(Thomson, supra, p. 649.) And the Director has a financial interest in the agreements because they 
involve a source of income.2 Therefore, Section 1090 prohibits Director Coates Hedberg from 
making or participating in the making of, and SDCW A from entering into, the contracts unless a 
remote or noninterest exception. 
Step Five: Does either a remote or nonintcrcst exception apply? 
As a general rule, when Section 1090 is applicable to one member of a governing body of a 
public entity, as here, the prohibition cannot be avoided by having the interested board member 
abstain; the entire governing body is precluded from entering into the contract. (Thomson, supra, at 
pp. 647-649; Stigall, supra, at p. 569; 86 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 138, 139 (2003); 70 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 
45, 48 (1987).) However, the Legislature has created various statutory exceptions to Section 1090's 
prohibition where the financial interest involved is deemed to be a "remote interest," as defined in 
Section 1091, or a "noninterest," as defined in Section 1091.5. 
Under the present circumstances, only the "remote interest" specified in Section 1091(b)(2) 
appears relevant. An official has a remote interest in a contract entered into by the body or board of 
which the official is a member if the official is an employee or agent of the contracting party and all 
of the following factors are present: 
• 
The contracting party has IO or more employees. 
• The employee has been an employee or agent of that party for at least 3 years prior to the 
official's term in office. 
• The employee owns less than 3 percent of the shares of stock of the contracting party. 
• The employee is not an officer or director of the contracting party. 
• The employee did not directly participate in formulating the bid of the contracting party. 
(See Section 109l(b)(2).) 
2 We note that as a general rule, a member of a board or commission always has a financial interest in his or 
her spouse's source of income for purposes of Section 1090. (See, e.g., 78 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 230, 235 ( 1995).) 


File No. A-17-174 
Page No. 4 
The facts you have provided do not fall literally within the language of the exception. 
Section 109l(b)(2) contemplates a relationship between the official and the contracting party as the 
basis for the conflict of interest. Here, Director Coates Hedberg is not an employee of the 
contracting party, but rather her spouse is an employee of the contracting party. Nonetheless, we 
believe the policy underlying the exception is still served by applying it to the present situation 
provided the five factors are satisfied. The facts you have provided indicate that all five factors are 
present. Accordingly, Director Coates Hedberg has a remote interest in the subject contracts. 
When a "remote interest" is present, the contract may be made if (I) the officer in question 
discloses his or her financial interest in the contract to the public agency, (2) such interest is noted 
in the entity's official records, and (3) the officer abstains from any participation in the making of 
the contract. (Section 109l(a); 88 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 106, 108 (2005); 83 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 246, 
248 (2000).) Therefore, SDCWA may enter into contracts with SDG&E, so long as the Director 
discloses her financial interest in the contracts, it is noted in the official records, and she abstains 
from any participation in making the contracts. 
Because we conclude that Director Coates Hedberg will have a "remote interest" in future 
contracts, we do not apply the remaining steps in the analysis. 
Government Code Section 87100 of the Act: 
Additionally, since the remedy in this situation is for her to abstain from any participation in 
the approval of such contracts due to her remote interest, we do not need to further analyze the 
conflict of interest issue raised under the Act. Director Coates Hedberg' s abstention under Section 
109I(a)would also satisfy the requirements of the Act. 
If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660. 
By: 
ZWN:jgl 
Sincerely, 
Brian G. Lau 
Assistant General Counsel 
Q 
Senior Counsel, Legal Division

sections.analysis:
Government Code Section 1090: 
Under Section l 090, "the prohibited act is the making of a contract in which the official has 
a financial interest." (People v. Honig (1996) 48 Cal.App.4th 289, 333.) A contract that violates 
Section 1090 is void. (Thomson v. Call (1985) 38 Cal.3d 633, 646.) Section 1090 is concerned with 
financial interests, other than remote or noninterests, that prevent public officials from exercising

and distinct from that applied under the Act.  
We employ a six-step analysis to determine whether an official has a disqualifying conflict 
of interest under Section 1090. These steps are: ( 1) does the matter involve a public officer or 
employee; (2) does the matter involve a contract; (3) is the official making or participating in

noninterest exception apply; and, under appropriate facts, (6) does the rule of necessity apply? 
Steps one through four are not at issue here. This matter involves the Director in her role as 
a public officer, and various contractual agreements with SDG&E concerning energy and water.

officer is a member of a board or commission that has the power to execute the contract at issue. 
(Thomson, supra, p. 649.) And the Director has a financial interest in the agreements because they 
involve a source of income.2 Therefore, Section 1090 prohibits Director Coates Hedberg from 
making or participating in the making of, and SDCW A from entering into, the contracts unless a 
remote or noninterest exception. 
Step Five: Does either a remote or nonintcrcst exception apply?

45, 48 (1987).) However, the Legislature has created various statutory exceptions to Section 1090's

• The employee owns less than 3 percent of the shares of stock of the contracting party. 
• The employee is not an officer or director of the contracting party. 
• The employee did not directly participate in formulating the bid of the contracting party. 
(See Section 109l(b)(2).) 
2 We note that as a general rule, a member of a board or commission always has a financial interest in his or 
her spouse's source of income for purposes of Section 1090. (See, e.g., 78 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 230, 235 ( 1995).)

Section 109l(b)(2) contemplates a relationship between the official and the contracting party as the

in the entity's official records, and (3) the officer abstains from any participation in the making of

the approval of such contracts due to her remote interest, we do not need to further analyze the
